summary: The v0.1.1 audit-closure change makes reviewer-identity metadata honestly nullable, adds real `claude --version` CLI detection, extracts `reviewer_model` from `modelUsage`, regenerates the Claude review adapter lockfile with sha256 hashes (restoring the pywin32 Windows marker), and adds targeted regression tests. The implementation is conservative and well-tested: schemas, templates, adapter, and tests are updated consistently; the `"unknown"` sentinel is replaced by `null`; CLI-version detection is separated from review execution and threaded through all run paths; and lockfile hash validation is both statically asserted and verified live behind an opt-in env gate. I found no critical, high, or correctness-breaking defects. Findings are limited to low/info robustness, determinism, and test-coverage observations, plus documented residual risks around downstream null consumers and the unverifiable model version.

findings.title: CLI version detection trusts the first non-empty line without shape validation
findings.evidence: `detect_claude_cli_version` returns the first non-empty stripped line from stdout/stderr of `claude --version` (`for line in _to_text(value).splitlines(): ... if text: return text`). If a CLI build ever prints a banner, telemetry notice, or warning before the version token, that line becomes the recorded `reviewer_cli_version`. The verification only confirms `2.1.168 (Claude Code)` for the current install.
findings.recommendation: Prefer a line matching a version-like regex (e.g. digits separated by dots) before falling back to the first non-empty line, or document that the recorded value is the raw first line and therefore advisory.

findings.title: modelUsage extraction records only the first key and is order-dependent
findings.evidence: `_model_usage_name` iterates `parsed.get("modelUsage")` and returns the first string key. When a review uses multiple models, which model is recorded as `reviewer_model` depends on JSON insertion order, and the actual reviewing model may not be the first one. The README documents 'first modelUsage key' but not the multi-model ambiguity.
findings.recommendation: Either aggregate all modelUsage keys (e.g. into a secondary field) or define an explicit selection rule (primary/longest-usage model) so recorded metadata is deterministic across runs that use multiple models.

findings.title: Lockfile hash correctness is only validated by an opt-in, network-dependent test
findings.evidence: `test_claude_review_adapter_lockfile_hash_validation_passes` is gated behind `HARNESS_RUN_PIP_HASH_CHECK=1` and shells out to real pip. The default suite (`test_claude_review_adapter_dependencies_are_locked`) only asserts that `--hash=sha256:` substrings are present, not that hashes match the pinned distributions. A corrupted or mistyped hash would pass default CI and only surface in opt-in runs.
findings.recommendation: Run the opt-in hash validation in the release/CI pipeline (or a periodic scheduled job) so hash regressions are caught before merge rather than only when a contributor remembers to set the env var.

findings.title: Synchronous version probe adds latency to every review path
findings.evidence: `run_claude_review` now calls `detect_claude_cli_version(claude_executable)` (10s timeout) before every review, including failure paths (auth_missing, nonzero, schema_invalid). For long-lived server runs this re-detects the version on every invocation.
findings.recommendation: Cache the detected version per process (or per executable mtime) and only re-probe when the cache is empty, since CLI version rarely changes within a process lifetime.

findings.title: reviewer identity ('claude-code') can diverge from the actual inference model
findings.evidence: `REVIEWER = "claude-code"` is hardcoded while real `modelUsage` exposes `glm-5.2[1m]` (a GLM-family model). Recording is honest, but a reader of the envelope could infer Claude executed the review when a backed/proxied model did.
findings.recommendation: Clarify in the schema/template docs that `reviewer` denotes the adapter/prompt identity (Claude Code CLI) while `reviewer_model` is the proven inference model, so the two are not conflated in audit trails.

findings.title: minLength:1 on nullable identity fields is correct but partially redundant
findings.evidence: Schemas now declare `"type": ["string", "null"], "minLength": 1` for model/model_version/cli_version and the reviewer_* fields. Under JSON Schema draft-07+ (jsonschema 4.26 is in use), `minLength` only constrains string instances, so null validates and empty strings are rejected. This is the intended 'non-empty-or-null' contract.
findings.recommendation: No change required; consider a one-line schema comment clarifying that minLength applies only to the string variant, to aid future maintainers unfamiliar with nullable-string constraints.

tested: Schema nullability for model/model_version/cli_version in state.schema.json and claude-review-output.schema.json (test_state_schema, test_static_contracts.test_claude_review_output_schema_allows_nullable_identity_metadata)
External review template now emits null for reviewer_model/reviewer_model_version/reviewer_cli_version
reviewer_model extracted from explicit 'model' field with precedence over modelUsage (test_normalize_prefers_explicit_model_metadata_over_model_usage)
reviewer_model extracted from first modelUsage key (test_normalize_extracts_model_from_model_usage)
Non-dict modelUsage (e.g. list) yields null reviewer_model (test_normalize_ignores_non_dict_model_usage)
reviewer_model_version remains null when no real version field exists
Detected CLI version wins over stale JSON metadata (test_normalize_prefers_detected_cli_version_over_output_metadata)
detect_claude_cli_version returns None on OSError, TimeoutExpired, nonzero returncode, and empty output (test_detect_claude_cli_version_returns_none_for_unavailable_outputs)
run_claude_review records detected CLI version and modelUsage metadata and calls claude --version first (test_run_records_detected_cli_version_and_model_usage_metadata)
Failure paths (schema_invalid, failed, auth_missing) now carry reviewer_cli_version and updated subprocess side-effect ordering
Lockfile pins exact versions with --hash=sha256 entries and preserves pywin32==312 Windows marker (test_claude_review_adapter_dependencies_are_locked)
Live pip --require-hashes --dry-run resolves the full lockfile closure when HARNESS_RUN_PIP_HASH_CHECK=1
README and mcp/claude-review/README.md updated to install with --require-hashes
Harness CLI validation passes for example-fast-doc-change, 2026-06-19-standard-adapter-cli, and this run; pip check clean; server import smoke ok
not_tested: Cross-platform lockfile install behavior outside Windows/Python 3.12 (only the current Windows env was exercised)
Clean virtual-environment install that bypasses pip's existing cache
Non-Windows execution of the opt-in pip hash integration test
Behavior when claude --version prints a non-version banner line first (no regex/shape validation in code or tests)
Multi-model modelUsage scenarios and determinism of which key is recorded as reviewer_model
Long-lived MCP client registration under Codex's MCP runtime
Full repository scan for downstream consumers of the now-nullable model/model_version/cli_version and reviewer_* fields (review was limited to the embedded diff)
residual_risks: Downstream code (review aggregation, state transitions, reporting, evidence rendering) that previously treated model/model_version/cli_version and reviewer_* as non-null strings could raise AttributeError/TypeError on null values; only schema, adapter, template, and tests are visible in this diff, so unshown consumers are unverified.
reviewer_model_version will remain null until the CLI or Claude output exposes a distinct, verifiable model-version field; current real logs only expose a model name via modelUsage.
The opt-in hash-validation test depends on network or pip cache availability and is skipped by default, so hash regressions are not caught in the default suite.
Lockfile was generated against Python 3.12 on Windows; resolver/hashes could differ for other interpreters or platforms despite the pywin32 marker.
Recorded reviewer_cli_version and reviewer_model are advisory strings derived from CLI/output text without structural validation, so malformed or re-ordered CLI output could be recorded verbatim.
